interactions.summary: Jiang says he teaches the great books as portals into divine consciousness and keeps speaking even with little success because the universe is watching, someone may hear, and he has a duty to his children to spread hope.
The host asks why Jiang chooses high school rather than university; Jiang starts by saying intellectual freedom matters most.
Jiang says universities have little freedom because tenure requires narrow conformity, while fundamental truth-seeking and broad inquiry are dismissed as madness.
The host praises teachers as sources of imagination, ambition, and serendipity and asks Jiang's message for developing-economy educators who want more open-minded humanist education.
Jiang's message is 'you matter': one person's free will and goodness affect the universe; refusing satanic bargains and choosing wisdom, virtue, and speech gives even a nobody impact.

speaker_notes.note: Segment includes host's question and Jiang's opening answer; diarization keeps it in one span.
Performed host question on education in developing economies; eligible as interview question.

claims.claim: Jiang says the great books are portals into the mind of the universe and lifelong guides to happiness, meaning, and purpose.
Jiang says high school offers more intellectual freedom than university because universities reward narrow conformity and punish fundamental inquiry.
Jiang says every person matters because cause and effect connect every act to the universe; choosing goodness and refusing power/fame bargains protects free will and creates impact.
claims.temporal_scope: Normative metaphysical claim in this interview.

signature_moments.moment: The great books become portals into divine consciousness, not prestige objects.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a portal into the mind of the universe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It states Jiang's educational mission in its strongest metaphysical form.

signature_moments.moment: The whole education answer collapses into two words: you matter.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the human answer to the interview's systems of compliance and despair.

signature_moments.moment: The devil cannot punish what it never bought; safety comes from refusing the bargain.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you have to let satan in the door
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It connects free will, anti-elite refusal, and courage under threat.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Plato, Homer, Dante, Milton, Shakespeare and similar works treated as portals into meaning, happiness, and divine consciousness.
Jiang's condensed message that free will and chosen goodness affect the whole interconnected universe.
Hermetic principle Jiang invokes to argue every choice affects everything else.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang says he has been trying to teach this great-books message for 20 to 30 years and only recently gained attention because current events force reflection.
